1.3
Low-degree polynomial framework In the low-degree polynomial framework, we only consider estimators, or test statistics, within the class of
multivariate polynomials of degree at most D of the data. Its premise is that, for a large class of problems, the
polynomials of degree D = O(log n) are as powerful as the best polynomial-time algorithms. Hence, proving
the failure of degree O(log n) polynomials is a strong indication [KWB19; Wei25] that no polynomial-time
algorithm can solve this task. This framework is closely related to other approaches including statistical
queries [Bre+20], free-energy landscapes from statistical physics [Ban+22] or approximate message pass-
ing [MW24]. Low-degree polynomials have been fruitfully applied to a large range of detection problems,
including community detection [HS17], spiked tensor models [HS17; KWB19] among many others. Although
it has originally been proposed for detection problems, it has been subsequently extended to estimation
problems [SW22; SW25; Car+25]. In order to ﬁt the clustering problem within the low-degree estimation framework, we need to introduce
a prior distribution on the parameters µk’s in and k∗’s of the model (1). We write P (resp. E) for the speciﬁc
marginal probability (resp. expectation) distribution of Y . Also, we need to reduce the clustering problem
to the problem of estimating a functional x ∈R. This will be made explicit in the beginning of Section 2,
but one may think of x as the indicator function that the ﬁrst and second rows belong to the same group,
so that the problem of recovering x is a sub-problem of our global clustering problem from Equation 2. For

For simple detection problems of a distribution P against Q considered e.g. in [Hop+17; KWB19], one
can bound the counterpart of the low-degree correlation (9) by considering an orthonormal basis of the space

of polynomials with respect to P, this allows to directly express the supremum in (9) as a sum of projec-
tions. When P corresponds to the distribution of independent Bernoulli random variables or independent
standard normal distributions, the canonical basis or the multivariate Hermite basis are easily shown to
be orthogonal. However, for generation estimation problem as considered here, the distribution P is not
a product distribution and no explicit orthonormal basis is known. This technical hurdle prevented the
development of low-degree lower bound for estimation problems. Schramm and Wein [SW22] proposed to
lower bound the denominator of (9) using Jensen’s trick, which then allows to control Corr≤D as a sum of
multivariate cumulants. This provided a versatile tool which has been applied among others to submatrix
estimation [SW22], stochastic block models and graphons [LG23], and Gaussian mixture models [EGV25].
However, for Gaussian mixture models, bounding cumulants does not allow to improve over the spectral-type
lower bound (4) from [EGV25]. More recently, Sohn and Wein [SW25] developed sharper theory, but solving
the corresponding overcomplete linear systems is extremely involved and we did not manage to apply this
approach for our problem.
As an alternative, Carpentier et al. [Car+25] have advocated for a more direct approach to lower bound
the risk of low-degree polynomials by constructing a family of polynomials that is almost orthogonal under P.
This has been applied to several planted problems in random graphs [Car+25] and in particular to stochastic
block models with many groups [CGV25]. One advantage is that such a basis provides strong intuition on
the form of optimal polynomials that match the lower bound (6). In this work, we extend and sharpen this
approach from random graph to Gaussian mixture models. In turn, this provides us insights to introduce
our new clustering procedures.

In this manuscript, we focus on the problem of estimating the partition

and clustering. Indeed, in a moderate-dimensional regime (n ≥dK) a consistent estimation of the µk’s is

Anisotropic Gaussian mixture models.
We underline that we focus in this work on the isotropic case
where the covariance matrices of the mixture is proportional to the identity matrix. In the non-isotropic
case, there is an additional statistical-computational gap that does not only arise when the number of K of
groups is large but also because of the unknown covariance structure. In particular, [DKS17] and [Dia+23b]
establish such gaps through the lens of the Statistical Query lower bounds

In this section, we describe important steps for the proof of Theorem 1. First, we introduce an almost-
orthogonal basis under P which is central in our arguments. After reducing the polynomial space by invariance
properties, we introduce a multigraph formalism that will be used to deﬁne the polynomials. Then, we
introduce a class of invariant polynomials that will be used to construct the estimator and to prove the lower
bound. We prove that it is suﬃcient to restrict to the vector sub-space generated by these polynomials.
Then, we correct this polynomial basis to establish its near orthonormality under P.

3.1

Conjecture and open problems.
As explained in the introduction, we conjecture that our low-degree
polynomial lower bounds is, up to polylog, sharp for general Gaussian mixture models. To establish Con-
jecture 1, we would need to introduce and analyze a polynomial-time procedure that is able to recover the
clusters as long as the square separation ∆∗satisﬁes

In this work, we only dealt with the case where the µk’s are either either orthogonal or opposite to each other.
We doubt that our procedure is able to deal with general µk’s. Although the iterative tensor projection
method of [LL22] does not allow to recover the desired condition, we believe that such an approach is a
promising research direction. Finally, our lower bound techniques are only tailored to d ≥K. For small
dimension problems d < K, pinpointing the optimal separation condition for polynomial-time procedures
would require new ideas.

Half-edge pairings.
Then, given a node matching M, we deﬁne a pairing P of the half-edges as a subset
of Ehalf,(1) × Ehalf,(2) such that (i) each half-edge in Ehalf,(1) or Ehalf,(2) occurs at most once, (ii) (e, e′)
belongs to P implies that (i(e), i(e′)) belongs to M. The second condition ensures that it is only possible
to match half-edges that are incident to a node that has been paired. Finally, we write P := P(M) for
the collection of all half-edge pairings associated to a node matching M. We also abreviate by MP for the
collections of all such matching of nodes and associated half-edges pairings of G(1) and G(2). Similarly, we
abreviate by M⋆P for the set of pairing and matching associated to the matchings M⋆(instead of M).

such that all half-edges adjacent to nodes in M are paired. This set is in fact empty, unless |V (1)| = |V (2),
all node are matched, and all matched nodes have the same degree.
We write Mfull for the set of pairs of matched nodes - namely in M - namely that have all half-edges
connected to them that are paired - namely in P. We say that these nodes are fully matched. Figure 3
displays an example of matching and pairing where one pair of nodes is fully matched.

Merged multigraph G∪[M; P].
We consider two templates G(1) and G(2), a node matching M ⊂V (1) ×

We write G(1)[M; P] (resp. G(2)[M; P]) the submultigraph G∪[M; P] that only contains the nodes and
the half-edges arising from G(1) (resp. G(2)). Obviously, G(1)[M; P] is isomorphic to G(1).

Paths and cycles.

cycles obtained when matching and pairing the multigraphs. We will mostly use its cardinality |Cyc|.

Construction of G∆.
Finally, we deﬁne G∆:= G∆[M, P] = (V∆, E∆) as the pruned multi-graph of
G∪[M, P] as follows: • We remove all the edges that belong to a cycle. • For any maximum open path with colored half-edges, we remove all these-half-edges and replace them
by an edge between the two extremities. Finally, we write CC(G∆) for the set of connected components of G∆. Figure 3 illustrates an example of
construction - note that other examples are found in Figures 5 and Figure 4.
